text: The laparoscopic view displays the operative field for the transperineal approach to address secondary rectal cancer.
The surgeon performs laparoscopic mobilization of the left-sided colon and ligates the inferior mesenteric artery, using precise instrument manipulation within the abdominal cavity.
During posterior dissection, the surgeon pushes up the posterior wall of the rectum, visibly extending the fibrous tissues in the operative field.
The laparoscopic view shows the left side of the operative field, focusing on the anatomical structures and tissue surrounding the rectal area.
The assistant and surgeon use triangulation with their hands to extend the fibrous tissues between the mesorectum and surrounding tissues, visible in the laparoscopic view.

The surgeon attempts to mobilize the mesorectum laparoscopically, encountering difficulty due to increased pelvic fibrosis visible in the operative field, a result of previous high-dose radiation therapy.
The surgical team transitions to a transperineal approach, preparing to access the rectal area from the perineum.
The surgeon makes a skin incision around the tightly closed anus and connects a gel point mini device to facilitate the transperineal approach.
The video displays the operative field's orientation in relation to the anus, visible in the lower left corner of the screen.
The surgeon divides the fat tissues in the ischioanal fossa, progressively exposing the underlying levator ani muscle.
The surgeon divides the levator ani muscle from the posterior side, continuing the dissection in the pelvic region.
The surgeon performs posterior presacral dissection, carefully separating tissue in the presacral space behind the rectum.
The surgeon pushes up the posterior side of the rectum, revealing and dividing the fibrous connective tissues covering the mesorectum.
The video displays an abdominal view in the upper right corner, providing a perspective of the surgical field from above.
The posterior dissection is connected with the dissection layer created by the conventional laparoscopic approach, showing continuity between the two surgical planes.
The surgeon completes the bilateral division of the levator ani muscle from the posterior side, further separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon extends the dissection procedure to the lateral side of the rectum, continuing to separate fibrous connective tissues covering the mesorectum.
The surgeon divides the levator ani muscle bilaterally from the posterior side, as seen in the surgical field.
During the lateral dissection, the surgeon identifies the neurovascular bundle as a landmark, ensuring precise separation of tissue layers in the rectal cancer procedure.
The surgeon performs perineal dissection, focusing on the anterior region, which is visually challenging due to its complexity.
The surgeon performs anterior dissection just behind the transverse perineal muscle, focusing on the area anterior to the rectum.
The surgeon identifies the arms of the puborectalis sling at the one and eleven o'clock positions, highlighting their anatomical orientation during the dissection.
The surgeon observes degenerative changes in the levator ani muscle, likely due to prior radiotherapy, as they continue the dissection in the pelvic region.
On the anterior side, the surgeon encounters increased fibrosis due to prior radiotherapy, making it difficult to identify a clear dissection layer.
The surgeon identifies the transverse perineal muscle during the dissection, highlighting its location in the surgical field.
The surgeon observes severe fibrous changes in the connective tissues in the area surrounding the previously identified transverse perineal muscle.
The surgical field shows severe fibrous changes in the connective tissues, likely due to prior radiotherapy, affecting the visibility and dissection of anatomical layers.
The surgeon identifies the neurovascular bundle, which serves as an important landmark for establishing the appropriate dissection line in the rectal cancer procedure.
The surgeon performs dissection near the neurovascular bundle, resulting in minor bleeding due to the proximity of the surgical action.
The surgeon adjusts the dissection line in response to visible bleeding points, ensuring careful navigation around the neurovascular bundle.
The video displays an abdominal view in the upper left corner, providing a visual perspective of the surgical field.
The laparoscopic view assists in determining the appropriate dissection line during lateral anterior dissection, providing a clear visual guide for precise surgical maneuvers.
The surgeon divides the perineal body at the inferior border of the prostate, using precise dissection techniques to separate the tissue.
The surgeon completes the rectal mobilization, indicating the end of this phase of the procedure.
The surgeon extracts the resected rectal specimen through the anus, completing the removal process.
The video shows a view from the transperineal side, highlighting the surgical field and anatomical structures visible from this perspective.
The pelvic splanchnic nerve, neurovascular bundle, and the cut end of the levator ani muscle are visible in the surgical field.
The video displays the perineal body, neurovascular bundle, prostate, and transverse perineal muscle in the surgical field.
